macro_segments.title: Non-Jewish Acquisition and the Mode of Transaction: Money vs. Physical Possession

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Recap of Non-Jewish Ownership and Its Exempting Effect
The Braita on Scrap Metal Purchase: Does Possession Without Payment or Payment Without Possession Effect a Transaction?
Gemara Analysis: If Money Acquires, Why Does Meshicha Matter? If Meshicha Acquires, Why Does Money Matter?
Abaye's Resolution: The Braita Discusses a Case Where Both Money and Meshicha Are Required Due to Stipulation
Rava's Alternative: Meshicha Effects the Transaction, but Money Determines the Halachic Consequences (Mekach Taos and Appearance)
Tosafot's Qualification: The Caveat Emptor Principle and When Mekach Taos No Longer Applies
Rav Ashi's Simple Reading: Money Alone Effects the Transaction; Meshicha Is Merely Mentioned Because It Was Discussed Earlier
Ravina's Counter-Position: Meshicha Effects the Transaction in Both Clauses of the Braita
The Status of Oral Commitments Between Jew and Non-Jew: Mechusrei Amana and the Gemarah's Observation on Trust
Summary: The Dominant Halachic Position and Its Practical Applications to Chametz, Shmita, and Bechor

macro_segments.title: Sacred Animals with Pre-Existing Blemishes: Limited Sanctity and Residual Status

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna on Sacred Animals: Overview of Two Contrasting Cases
Case 1: Blemish Precedes Sanctification—Sanctity Never Attaches; Redemption Fully Desacralizes
Implications of No Kedushat Korban: Exemption from Firstborn, Matanot, and Restrictions on Shearing and Work
Permitted Acts After Redemption: Shearing, Working, Keeping Offspring and Milk
The Exception of Bechor and Maaser: Why These Animals Retain Korban Sanctity Even with Pre-Existing Blemishes
Case 2: Sanctification Precedes Blemish—Korban Sanctity Attaches; Residual Status Remains After Redemption
Implications of Residual Korban Status: Ongoing Exemption from Firstborn and Matanot Even After Redemption
Restrictions Continue After Redemption: Prohibition on Shearing, Working, and Consuming Offspring and Milk
Shechita Outside the Mikdash and Temurah Permissibility: Consequences of Retaining Korban Sanctity
Gemara's Opening Question: Does Exemption from Bechor Depend Solely on Prior Redemption?

macro_segments.title: Sanctity and Exemption: How Kedushat Damim Exempts from Bechor Even Without Kedushat Korban

micro_segments.title: The Gemara Clarifies: Exemption from Bechor Occurs While the Animal Still Has Any Sanctity, Not Just After Redemption
Example of Non-Korban Sanctity: A Donkey Sanctified for Temple Treasury Cannot Give Birth to an Obligatory Peter Chamor
Why Shearing and Working Are Forbidden Before Redemption: Appearance Problem and Support for Rabbi Elazar's Position on Treasury Sanctity
Distinction Between Full Korban Sanctity and Treasury Sanctity: Why Our Case Looks More Like Korban

macro_segments.title: Offspring and Milk of Sanctified Animals: Status Before and After Redemption

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Simple Case: Offspring and Milk Are Permitted Only After Redemption
Gemara's Clarification: The Case Is Pregnancy Before Redemption, Birth After Redemption
Question About Offspring Born to a Blemished Animal: Can They Be Redeemed Without a Blemish Themselves?
Source Teaching: Offspring of Blemished Animals Sanctified for the Altar Can Be Sold and Need No Blemish
Principle Applied: The Derivative Should Not Be Stricter Than the Source

macro_segments.title: Automatic Upgrade of Sanctity: When Attempting to Sanctify for Treasury Elevates an Animal to Korban Status

micro_segments.title: Contrasting Case: Attempting to Sanctify a Perfect Animal for Treasury Value Only—Does It Automatically Become Korban Sanctity?
Gemara's Support for Rava: When Intention Is Treasury But Reality Permits Altar Use, the Animal Automatically Becomes a Korban
Rashi's Qualification: The Upgrade Applies When the Treasury Sanctity Is Inherently Directed Toward Procurement of a Korban

macro_segments.title: Extended Discussion on Blemishes and Sanctity: Why Certain Animals Retain Korban Status Despite Blemishes and Cannot Be Offered

micro_segments.title: Starting Point: If a Blemished Animal Is Slaughtered Outside the Mikdash, One Is Generally Exempt
Rabbi Eliezer's Dissent: The Verse Teaches That Even a Private Altar Prohibits Offering a Blemished Animal
Hermeneutical Chain: Excluding Other Cases via Extra Verses and Gezera Shava to Establish That Bechor, Maaser, Temura, and Offspring Cannot Be Offered with Blemishes
The Unified Principle: All Animals Possessing Kedushat Korban Irrespective of Blemish Cannot Actually Be Offered While Blemished

topical_tags.term: psulei mukdashin (redeemed blemished sacrifices with residual sanctity)
Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbinic majority on blemished offerings
Rava's position on meshicha and mekach taos
Tosafot's distinction and caveat emptor principle
